schools.summary: A disciple (perhaps Peter, Mark alone giving the words) admires the temple's prodigious marble stones (Josephus); they measured by size, not the widow's mite (Ellicott).
Bede: Christ leaves the temple, foretelling its ruin as Judaea's punishment; his departure ends the edifice of the law.
Henry: disciples idolize what looks great; Christ values not external pomp without purity — he weeps over souls, not magnificent houses.
Christ foretells complete demolition — 'not one stone upon another,' fulfilled in A.D. 70 and Hadrian's razing (Theophylact); only foundation stones remain (Robertson).
Henry: the temple's strength lay in its great stones — thrown down, no remembrance remains; the admired magnificence utterly overthrown.

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on the names Jesus and Christ, is an off-topic catechetical aside, not on this verse.

schools.summary: On Olivet, opposite the doomed temple (where Christ had wept, Luke 19:41), the four inner disciples — named only by Mark — ask privately, opening the discourse.
Bede: the Mount of Olives setting fits the words, figuring the Church's sublimity; Augustine frames the discourse as embracing Jerusalem, the Church's age, and the final end.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on 'It is I' in the storm (Mark 6), belongs elsewhere, not this verse.

themes: mount of Olives over against the temple

schools.summary: The two-fold question (when? what sign?); the disciples confound the temple's fall with the end of the world; Christ blends his death, Jerusalem's fall, and the second coming — threads not sharply separated (Robertson, Wesley).
Cyril: the apostles ask by divine arrangement to be guarded against the false Antichrist — yet 'as to the time let no one be curious.'

debates.question: What does the Olivet Discourse refer to?

sides.position: The destruction of Jerusalem (A.D. 70) and the end of the world, blended/typified
Chiefly the destruction of Jerusalem (the disciples' question)

schools.summary: Christ opens with a warning against deception that runs through the whole discourse and every age — men ever apply the picture to their own time (Robertson).
Henry: Christ directs consciences, not curiosity — warnings against deceivers, alarm at wars, and being drawn away by sufferings; 'when many are deceived, look to ourselves.'

themes: take heed lest any man deceive you

schools.summary: False christs claim the Messiah's dignity and deceive many — the judgment on Jews who rejected the true Christ (Henry), arising both before and after Jerusalem's fall.
Cyril: already fulfilled in part — Simon Magus, Menander, and the heresiarchs; others to come, culminating in Antichrist.

themes: many shall come in my name
judgment on rejecting the true Christ

schools.summary: Wars and rumours (fulfilled before A.D. 70) must come but are not yet the end; against the Jewish Gog-and-Magog expectation (Lightfoot), Christ steadies his people.
Henry: no surprise, no terror, no false inference that the end is near — believers may enjoy serenity amid the world's disorder.

themes: the end shall not be yet

schools.summary: Wars, earthquakes, famines (documented between A.D. 30-70) are 'the beginnings of sorrows' — birth-pangs (Barnes), the painful prelude, not the end; cf. Isaiah 66:7-8 (Lightfoot).
Cyril: these signs come to pass — 'Watch therefore, for ye know not at what hour your Lord doth come.'
Persecution before councils, synagogues, rulers, and kings (fulfilled in Acts); Mark's emphatic 'take heed to yourselves'; the trials become a testimony and a pulpit (Henry).
Bede: tormenting the heralds was a cause of Judaea's ruin, yet companions in the kingdom shall not be wanting; 'for a testimony against them' leaves persecutors inexcusable (Theophylact).

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on the popular enthusiasm, belongs elsewhere, not this verse.

schools.summary: Mark alone gives this form: the gospel 'must first' be published to all nations before the end — fulfilled in the Roman world before A.D. 70 (Paul, Colossians 1:6, 23); a divine necessity no persecution can thwart.

themes: the gospel must first be published

schools.summary: The Spirit's help concerns defences before tribunals (fulfilled in Acts), not ordinary preaching (no excuse for the lazy preacher, Robertson); the apostles' defences are 'the words of the Holy Ghost' (Barnes).
Calvin: designed to relieve anxiety and excite trust (not indolence) — not deliverance from death but the Spirit guiding their tongues to confound adversaries with a wise reply.

Betrayal even by closest kin — brother, father, children — to death, the hatred of the gospel overcoming natural affection (Barnes), fulfilling Micah 7:6; forewarned to steel the disciples for the bitterest trial (Theophylact).

Hated of all men for Christ's name, yet 'he that endureth to the end shall be saved' — final salvation (Robertson); the cause, not the suffering, makes the martyr (Theophylact); perseverance gains the crown (Henry).

themes: hated of all men for my name's sake

schools.summary: The 'abomination of desolation' (Daniel 9:27) = the Roman armies compassing Jerusalem (Luke 21:20, a second fulfilment of Daniel); the sign for the Christians to flee, fulfilled in their escape to Pella.
Bede: variously Caesar's/Hadrian's statue, or Antichrist; the Christians, warned, fled beyond Jordan to Pella; a layered fulfilment extending to the future Antichrist (Cyril, 2 Thessalonians 2).
Henry: the Roman armies standing where they ought not; flight urgent — save your life though you save nothing else.

The man on the housetop must flee at once, not descending for his goods — time more precious than possessions (Henry); better saved naked than perish encumbered (Gloss); life outweighs property in the hour of judgment.

themes: him that is on the housetop

schools.summary: The man in the field must not turn back even for his garment but flee as he is (Henry) — the same urgency as v.15, now on the labourer; town and country alike, the response is immediate, unencumbered flight.

themes: him that is in the field
not turn back for his garment

schools.summary: Gill: woe to pregnant and nursing women, hindered from swift flight and exposed to the enemy; the greatest comforts (child in womb, babe at breast) become the heaviest burdens in the day of calamity (Henry).

Pray the flight be not in winter (cold, short days, bad roads, no shelter); even certain trouble may be prayed about as to its circumstances (Henry); Mark omits Matthew's 'nor on the Sabbath' for Gentile readers (Ellicott).

themes: pray your flight be not in winter

schools.summary: Unequalled affliction (siege, zealots, famine), fulfilled in A.D. 70 as Josephus records; the amplified phrase from Daniel 12:1; Wesley asks if much is still to come.
Augustine: the unique tribulation is of the Jewish people; the future tribulation under Antichrist is a different reference; Cyril: confined by mercy to a few days.

The days mercifully shortened, else no flesh would survive — 'for the elect's sake, whom he hath chosen' (Mark's stress on sovereign election); the believing remnant the reason the storm subsided (Henry).
Theophylact: shortened for the sake of the believing Jews and those who would yet believe — God would not let the whole race be utterly destroyed.

themes: except the Lord had shortened those days

schools.summary: The renewed warning shifts ('then,' indefinitely) to Antichrist's time, when Satan unchained works lying wonders (Augustine); the elect, by definition, cannot finally be seduced though they tremble (Gregory); supremely Antichrist (Bede, Cyril; 2 Thessalonians 2).
A warning against false christs who would say 'Lo, here'; believe them not, for the true Christ comes visibly, not secretly.

themes: lo, here is Christ; believe him not

schools.summary: False christs and prophets with plausible signs and wonders (before and after A.D. 70, and supremely Antichrist); 'if it were possible, even the elect' both measures the danger and guarantees the elect cannot finally fall (Henry, Gregory).

Mark's repeated 'take ye heed'; the believer's safety in heeding Christ's complete forewarning ('I have foretold you all things') — gullibility no virtue (Robertson); the sufficiency of his word guards against deception (Henry).

themes: I have foretold you all things

schools.summary: The darkened sun and moon (from Isaiah, Ezekiel, Joel); Robertson cautions apocalyptic imagery need not be literal (Peter applied Joel to Pentecost) — the reference is part of the discourse debate (v.4).
Henry (with Wesley): these verses point to the second coming after the tribulation, rectifying the disciples' confusion; the lights literally outshone by the Son of man's glory.
Cyril: the world passes that the fairer world may appear — the cosmic signs mark the end and the coming of the Judge.

debates.question: Do the cosmic signs (vv.24-25) mark the literal second coming or figure the end of the Jewish order?

sides.position: The literal second coming and end of the world, after the tribulation
Apocalyptic imagery, possibly figurative of great upheaval

schools.summary: Falling stars and shaken powers — the prophets' language of cosmic dissolution for great judgments (Isaiah 13; Joel; Revelation 6); the heavens convulsed at the Judge's coming, or figuring an old order's overthrow (Gill).

The Son of Man seen coming in the clouds with power and glory (Daniel 7:13; Revelation 1:7); Mark's simpler form explains Matthew's 'sign'; Gill reads it figuratively of judgment on Jerusalem, others of the literal advent.
Augustine: he comes on a cloud as he ascended (Acts 1:11), both daily through the Church and at the final advent; Cyril: the sign of the Cross goes before the King.

themes: the Son of man coming in the clouds

schools.summary: The angels gather the elect from the four winds, earth's farthest bounds — the purpose of God through the ages (Robertson); Mark omits Matthew's trumpet; Gill reads it of the post-A.D. 70 Gentile ingathering.
Bede/Theophylact: the elect gathered from all the world to meet the Lord in the air (1 Thessalonians 4:17) — none missing, none miscarrying (Henry).
The budding fig tree presages summer, so the signs Christ gave presage the predicted events; applied to Jerusalem's ruin 'at the door' (Henry).
Bede: the fig tree figures the barren synagogue, fruitful when the fulness of the Gentiles comes in (Romans 11); Theophylact: summer follows the leaves as Christ's coming follows Antichrist's woes.

themes: learn a parable of the fig tree

schools.summary: Gill: the parable applied — when the foretold signs appear, the predicted event (Jerusalem's ruin, and in the layered reading the Son of Man's coming) is 'nigh, even at the doors'; sign and event as inseparable as the fig's leaves and summer (Henry).

'This generation shall not pass' — most naturally the contemporary generation, tying the prophecy to the ~38 years before A.D. 70 (Clarke); others read 'the Jewish race' (or Christians) because of verses looking beyond Jerusalem; part of the discourse debate (v.4).

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on the ways of gaining eternal life, is a broader catechetical aside, not on this verse.

themes: till all these things be done

schools.summary: Heaven and earth (the most enduring things) shall pass away, but Christ's word shall not — the seal of certainty on the prophecy (Henry; Isaiah 40:8); the immoveable elements fail before his word (Theophylact); a claim of divine authority.

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on the world's renewal, touches the theme but is a broader catechetical aside.

themes: heaven and earth shall pass away
my words shall not pass away

schools.summary: The precise time is hidden; 'neither the Son' applies to his finite human nature (as he grew in wisdom, Luke 2:52) — as God he knows all (Wesley, Barnes, Tillotson via Henry); some read the verb as 'make known' (Clarke); Robertson: it applies to the second coming.
Hilary: Christ cannot truly be ignorant — the treasures of wisdom are hidden in him; his 'ignorance' is an economy of not revealing (so Theophylact: he wisely prevents their asking).

debates.question: How can 'neither the Son' be reconciled with Christ's deity?

sides.position: The limitation belongs to his human nature; as God he knows all
An economy of not revealing; the hidden treasures of wisdom in him
The verb means 'make known' — not revealed by the Son but the Father

themes: of that day and hour knoweth no man
neither the Son, but the Father

schools.summary: The hidden hour's practical lesson: 'take ye heed, watch and pray' (Mark's refrain); the time concealed that the Church be always on guard — what may come any day must be prepared for every day (Henry).

themes: take ye heed, watch and pray
ye know not when the time is

schools.summary: The parable (only in Mark) of the absent master: Christ ascended, the Church his household, each servant with his work, the porter charged to watch — perhaps Peter, the keeper of the keys (Ellicott).
Bede: Christ left his Church bodily at the Ascension but keeps her by his divine presence; Gregory: the porter = the pastors, and all must watch the doors of their hearts.

themes: a man taking a far journey

schools.summary: Watch, for the Lord of the house may come in any of the four night watches (even, midnight, cockcrow, morning); the time undefined (2 Peter 3); applicable to death and the judgment, to any age of life (Henry, Theophylact).

themes: the four watches of the night
ye know not when he cometh

schools.summary: The danger: found sleeping (inattentive, negligent, unprepared) at the sudden return; the sleeping porter deserves punishment (Clarke); the coming is sudden, 'as a thief' (Henry) — spiritual sleep is fatal.
The closing 'Watch,' spoken to the four, is extended to all in every age (Henry); for each, the day of death is the Lord's coming (Augustine, Barnes) — the universal, urgent climax of the discourse.

themes: what I say unto you I say unto all
